---
title: "Yahoo Stock Price Prediction Pipeline"
author: "John Njuguna"
date: "2026-07-18"
output: html_document
---

## Executive Summary

This is an end-to-end framework implements structured time-series data cleaning, feature engineering, and a machine learning project to predict daily returns.

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE)
```

## Machine Learning Production Code

```{r}
         
# ==============================================================================
# Yahoo Stock Price Prediction Pipeline (R Translation)
# ==============================================================================

# Load necessary libraries
library(tidyverse)    # For data manipulation (dplyr, stringr, readr) and plotting (ggplot2)
library(changepoint)  # For PELT structural break analysis
library(lightgbm)     # For LightGBM Regressor
library(xgboost)      # For XGBoost Regressor
library(randomForest) # For Random Forest
library(e1071)        # For Support Vector Regression (SVR)
library(glmnet)       # For Ridge Regression
library(TTR)          # For RSI calculation helper
library(ggplot2)

# Load raw dataset
stock_df <- read_csv("data/raw/yahoo_stock.csv")
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1: Data Cleaning
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Convert Date column from text string to true Date object
stock_df <- stock_df %>% mutate(Date = as.Date(Date))

# Convert all column names to lowercase and swap spaces for underscores
colnames(stock_df) <- colnames(stock_df) %>% 
  str_trim() %>% 
  str_to_lower() %>% 
  str_replace_all(" ", "_")

# Sort chronologically by date
stock_df <- stock_df %>% arrange(date)

# Filter out weekend rows (Saturday = 6, Sunday = 7 in R's wday; or check weekday format)
# Using POSIXlt format where 0-6 represents Sunday-Saturday:
stock_df <- stock_df %>% 
  filter(!(as.POSIXlt(date)$wday %in% c(0, 6)))

# Summary Data Quality Report Function
data_quality_report <- function(df) {
  cat("Missing Values:\n")
  print(colSums(is.na(df)))
  cat("\nData Types:\n")
  print(sapply(df, class))
  cat("\nDuplicate Rows:\n")
  print(sum(duplicated(df$date)))
  cat("\nSummary Stats:\n")
  print(summary(df))
}

data_quality_report(stock_df)

# Outlier Management using 20-day rolling Z-score
# R matrix operations handle windows cleanly via slider or custom roll-apply functions
roll_mean_20 <- zoo::rollapplyr(stock_df$adj_close, width = 20, FUN = mean, fill = NA)
roll_std_20  <- zoo::rollapplyr(stock_df$adj_close, width = 20, FUN = sd, fill = NA)

z_scores <- (stock_df$adj_close - roll_mean_20) / roll_std_20
cat("Detected", sum(abs(z_scores) > 3, na.rm = TRUE), "localized outliers.\n")

# Define bounds and clip extreme values
upper_bound <- roll_mean_20 + (3 * roll_std_20)
lower_bound <- roll_mean_20 - (3 * roll_std_20)

stock_df$adj_close <- pmax(pmin(stock_df$adj_close, upper_bound), lower_bound)
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 2 & 3: Feature Engineering & EDA
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Shifts and computes mathematical stationary fields
stock_df <- stock_df %>% 
  mutate(
    prev_close = lag(adj_close, 1),
    daily_return = log(adj_close / prev_close),
    daily_range = high - low
  ) %>% 
  drop_na() # Drops rows containing initial NaNs

# Skewness validation
returns_skew <- e1071::skewness(stock_df$daily_return)
cat(sprintf("Daily Returns Skewness: %.4f\n", returns_skew))

# Visualizing Log Return Distribution (Testing Loss Aversion)
ggplot(stock_df, aes(x = daily_return)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 100, fill = "#DC143C", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkred", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(stock_df$daily_return, na.rm = TRUE), color = "black", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Daily Returns Distribution Profile (Testing Loss Aversion Loss Tails)",
       x = "Log Return Value", y = "Frequency (Days)") +
  theme_minimal()

# Volatility Autocorrelation (ACF) - Testing Panic Clustering
acf(abs(stock_df$daily_return), lag.max = 40, main = "Volatility Autocorrelation (Testing Panic Clustering Waves)")

# Compute rolling structural break variables
stock_df$rolling_volatility_20 <- zoo::rollapplyr(stock_df$daily_return, width = 20, FUN = sd, fill = NA)

# Structural Breaks detection via PELT algorithm
signal <- stock_df$daily_return
pelt_model <- cpt.meanvar(signal, method = "PELT", penalty = "Manual", pen.value = 5, test.stat = "Normal")
break_indices <- cpts(pelt_model)
break_dates <- stock_df$date[break_indices]

for(b_date in break_dates) {
  cat(sprintf("Structural Break Located: %s\n", as.Date(b_date, origin = "1970-01-01")))
}

# Behavioral Context Correlation Heatmap
stock_df <- stock_df %>% 
  mutate(
    return_lag_1 = lag(daily_return, 1),
    volume_lag_1 = lag(volume, 1)
  )

behavioral_cols <- c('daily_return', 'return_lag_1', 'daily_range', 'volume', 'volume_lag_1')
corr_matrix <- cor(stock_df %>% select(all_of(behavioral_cols)), use = "complete.obs")
print(round(corr_matrix, 2))

# Generate a clean line plot tracing out rolling metric shifts
ggplot(stock_df, aes(x = date, y = rolling_volatility_20)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkorange", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Market Volatility Over Time (Scanning for Structural Breaks / Crisis Shifts)",
    x = "Date Timeline",
    y = "Standard Deviation (Risk)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "top"
  )
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 4: Extended Feature Engineering (Modelling Setup)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

stock_df_expanded <- stock_df %>% 
  mutate(
    return_lag_2 = lag(daily_return, 2),
    return_lag_3 = lag(daily_return, 3),
    return_lag_4 = lag(daily_return, 4),
    return_lag_5 = lag(daily_return, 5),
    volume_lag_2 = lag(volume, 2),
    daily_range_lag_1 = lag(daily_return, 1), # Assigned matching python variable
    daily_range_lag_2 = lag(daily_range, 2),
    
    # Risk Tracking windows
    rolling_volatility_5  = zoo::rollapplyr(daily_return, width = 5, FUN = sd, fill = NA),
    rolling_volatility_60 = zoo::rollapplyr(daily_return, width = 60, FUN = sd, fill = NA)
  )

# RSI 14-day calculation from returns
# Using TTR package's standard implementation matching python logic structure
stock_df_expanded$rsi_14 <- RSI(stock_df_expanded$adj_close, n = 14)
stock_df_expanded$rsi_14_lag_1 <- lag(stock_df_expanded$rsi_14, 1)

# Clear matrix of nested Lookback NaN artifacts
stock_df_expanded <- stock_df_expanded %>% drop_na()
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 5: Train, Test, Split
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

expanded_features <- c(
  'return_lag_1', 'return_lag_2', 'return_lag_3', 'return_lag_4', 'return_lag_5',
  'volume_lag_1', 'volume_lag_2',
  'daily_range_lag_1', 'daily_range_lag_2',
  'rolling_volatility_5', 'rolling_volatility_20', 'rolling_volatility_60',
  'rsi_14_lag_1'
)

X <- stock_df_expanded %>% select(all_of(expanded_features))
y <- stock_df_expanded$daily_return

test_size <- 30
split_idx <- nrow(stock_df_expanded) - test_size

# Split matrices
X_train <- X[1:split_idx, ]
y_train <- y[1:split_idx]
X_test  <- X[(split_idx + 1):nrow(X), ]
y_test  <- y[(split_idx + 1):length(y)]
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 6: Scaling
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Custom MinMax Scaling mapping to prevent leakages
mins <- apply(X_train, 2, min)
maxs <- apply(X_train, 2, max)

X_train_scaled <- as.matrix(scale(X_train, center = mins, scale = maxs - mins))
X_test_scaled  <- as.matrix(scale(X_test, center = mins, scale = maxs - mins))
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 7: Modeling & Evaluation (Stable Production Matrix)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Metrics Calculation helper function
calculate_metrics <- function(actual, predicted) {
  rmse <- sqrt(mean((actual - predicted)^2)) * 100
  mae  <- mean(abs(actual - predicted)) * 100
  return(c(RMSE = rmse, MAE = mae))
}

# Model A: Linear Ridge Regression
model_ridge <- glmnet::glmnet(X_train_scaled, y_train, alpha = 0, lambda = 1.0)
preds_ridge <- as.vector(predict(model_ridge, X_test_scaled))

# Model B: Random Forest Regressor
model_rf <- randomForest::randomForest(X_train_scaled, y_train, ntree = 100, maxnodes = 2^4, nodesize = 10)
preds_rf <- predict(model_rf, X_test_scaled)

# Model C: Support Vector Regressor (SVR)
model_svr <- e1071::svm(X_train_scaled, y_train, type = "eps-regression", kernel = "radial", cost = 1.0, epsilon = 0.001)
preds_svr <- predict(model_svr, X_test_scaled)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LightGBM Regressor Implementation
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Format matrices into lightgbm's native dataset structure
dtrain <- lightgbm::lgb.Dataset(data = X_train_scaled, label = y_train)

# Exact hyperparameter tuning params from your original pipeline
params <- list(
  objective = "regression", 
  metric = "rmse", 
  learning_rate = 0.05, 
  max_depth = 4
)

# Train the tree booster model engine
model_lgb <- lightgbm::lgb.train(params = params, data = dtrain, nrounds = 100, verbose = -1)

# Generate predictions on unseen out-of-sample data
preds_lgb <- predict(model_lgb, X_test_scaled)


# Naive Benchmark baseline
preds_naive <- rep(0.0, length(y_test))

# Generate Model Leaderboard Evaluation Summary Statistics
metrics_matrix <- rbind(
  "LightGBM"                   = calculate_metrics(y_test, preds_lgb),
  "Support Vector Machine SVR" = calculate_metrics(y_test, preds_svr),
  "Random Forest (Bagging)"    = calculate_metrics(y_test, preds_rf),
  "Naive Mean Model"           = calculate_metrics(y_test, preds_naive),
  "Ridge Regression (Linear)"  = calculate_metrics(y_test, preds_ridge)
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Evaluation Visualization: True Out-of-Sample SVR Forecast Tracking
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Extract matching evaluation timeline dimensions
test_dates <- stock_df_expanded$date[(split_idx + 1):nrow(stock_df_expanded)]

# Construct clean data frames utilizing your active mathematical SVR predictions
# (Multiplied by 100 to explicitly format as true percentages)
svr_plot_df <- data.frame(
  date      = test_dates,
  Actual    = as.numeric(y_test) * 100,
  Predicted = as.numeric(preds_svr) * 100 # Computed live by your e1071 SVR model
)

# Render the output as a beautiful table layout directly in the document
knitr::kable(round(metrics_matrix, 4), caption = "Model Performance Leaderboard Matrix (Scores in %)")
```

```{r}
# Render the SVR time-series overlay visualization
ggplot(svr_plot_df, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray", linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) +
  # Actual Returns (Black solid line with round points)
  geom_line(aes(y = Actual, color = "Actual Daily Return"), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Actual, color = "Actual Daily Return"), size = 1.5) +
  # SVR Predictions (Crimson dot-dash line with triangle points)
  geom_line(aes(y = Predicted, color = "SVR (RBF Kernel) Predicted Return"), linetype = "dotdash", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Predicted, color = "SVR (RBF Kernel) Predicted Return"), shape = 17, size = 1.5) +
  # Labeling and hex color assignments matching your python configuration
  labs(
    title = "Out-of-Sample SVR Forecast Evaluation (Precision Tracking Horizon)",
    x = "Date Timeline",
    y = "Daily Return Percentage (%)"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name = NULL,
    values = c("Actual Daily Return" = "black", "SVR (RBF Kernel) Predicted Return" = "#DC143C")
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
  )

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Evaluation Visualization: True Out-of-Sample Daily Forecast Tracking
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 1. Dynamically extract the exact test dates
test_dates <- stock_df_expanded$date[(split_idx + 1):nrow(stock_df_expanded)]

# 2. Extract the exact mathematically computed predictions from your model
# (We scale both vectors by 100 to display them as percentages)
plot_df <- data.frame(
  date      = test_dates,
  Actual    = as.numeric(y_test) * 100,
  Predicted = as.numeric(preds_lgb) * 100  # Dynamically generated by LightGB
)

# 3. Render the true time-series forecasting overlay visualization
ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray", linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) +
  # Actual Returns Line
  geom_line(aes(y = Actual, color = "Actual Daily Return"), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Actual, color = "Actual Daily Return"), size = 1.5) +
  # Exact Model Predictions Line (No manual data estimation)
  geom_line(aes(y = Predicted, color = "LightGB Model Predicted Return"), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Predicted, color = "LightGB Model Predicted Return"), shape = 15, size = 1.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Out-of-Sample LightGB Forecast Evaluation (Next 30 Trading Days)",
    subtitle = "Generated Dynamically via Production Machine Learning Framework",
    x = "Date Timeline",
    y = "Daily Return Percentage (%)"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name = NULL,
    values = c("Actual Daily Return" = "black", "LightGB Model Predicted Return" = "darkorange")
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
  )
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 8: LightGBM Feature Importance Analysis
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Extract feature tree split gain profiles natively from LightGBM
tree_imp <- lightgbm::lgb.importance(model_lgb, percentage = TRUE)

# Print data summary report table layout directly in the document
knitr::kable(tree_imp, digits = 4, caption = "LightGBM Feature Gain Profiles")

# Render a stunning portfolio chart using the exact LightGBM metrics matrix
ggplot(tree_imp, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Gain), y = Gain)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "darkcyan", width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "LightGBM Feature Importance Profile",
    subtitle = "Calculated via Percentage Gain Allocation Matrix",
    x = "Model Feature Fields",
    y = "Relative Feature Gain Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
